text.title: Cloud Run Vpc Access Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules for the cloud-run-vpc-access methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale + concrete remediation hook.

rule.statement: Reach private resources via either a Serverless VPC Access connector (gen1) or direct VPC egress (gen2). Pick exactly one — do not mix.
rule.rationale: Mixing connector and direct egress creates undefined routing precedence and intermittent failures.

rule.statement: Set `--vpc-egress=private-ranges-only` whenever the service only needs to reach private CIDRs; reserve `all-traffic` for services that also need internet egress through VPC.
rule.rationale: `all-traffic` routes every packet through VPC NAT, inflating cost and pulling internet traffic through customer firewalls unnecessarily.

rule.statement: Serverless VPC Access connectors MUST be sized with min/max instances appropriate to throughput; the connector subnet must be a dedicated `/28`.
rule.rationale: Undersized connectors throttle egress and silently degrade tail latency; shared subnets risk IP exhaustion.

rule.statement: Internal-only services MUST set `--ingress=internal` (or `internal-and-cloud-load-balancing`) to block public traffic at the platform level.
rule.rationale: Relying on IAM alone leaves a publicly reachable endpoint that can be probed; ingress=internal removes the attack surface entirely.

rule.statement: Multiple Cloud Run services in the same region MAY share a single connector; do not create a connector per service.
rule.rationale: Per-service connectors waste IP space and quota; sharing is the supported pattern when throughput requirements stay within the connector's capacity.

rule.statement: If the input fails ALL "Applies If" preconditions or matches ANY "Skip If" disqualifier, do not apply this methodology — route the task to the adjacent sibling instead.
rule.rationale: Even flat checklists need an explicit skip leaf in the decision tree; this rule is the universal terminal.
